era_context: Late Bronze Age; Ramesses II in his early-to-middle reign; Hittite Empire under Muwatalli II; Mycenaean Greece in Late Helladic IIIB; Shang Dynasty approaching its end (traditionally dated to 1046 BCE, though some scholars propose earlier dates). This period represents the height of Late Bronze Age civilization before the systemic collapse of c. 1200 BCE.

events.title: Ramesses II's military campaigns and diplomatic relations
events.description: Ramesses II maintained active military campaigns in the Levant and Nubia during this period, securing Egyptian interests and tribute. Diplomatic correspondence with the Hittite Empire and other Near Eastern powers continued, establishing the framework for the later peace treaty (c. 1259 BCE). Egypt's military and economic power remained at its height.

sources.name: Temple reliefs, administrative papyri, and later historical records

events.certainty_note: Ramesses II's military activity is well-documented in general; specific events in 1287 BCE are not individually attested but fit the pattern of his reign.

events.title: Hittite Empire under Muwatalli II maintaining regional power
events.description: Muwatalli II continued to consolidate Hittite power and maintain the empire's position as a major Near Eastern force. The Hittites controlled much of Anatolia and contested Egyptian influence in the Levant. This period of Hittite strength would culminate in the confrontation with Ramesses II at Kadesh (c. 1275 BCE).

sources.name: Hittite royal inscriptions and clay tablets

events.certainty_note: Muwatalli II's reign is dated c. 1295–1272 BCE; this year falls within his rule, but no specific events are attested for 1287 BCE itself.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the absolute chronology of the Late Bronze Age remains debated; some scholars advocate for a 'low chronology' that would shift these dates by 50–100 years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1287 BCE is limited to the major literate civilizations of the Near East. Egyptian sources dominate the surviving documentation, creating a bias toward Egyptian perspectives and activities. Hittite sources provide an alternative viewpoint but are similarly state-centric. The broader Mediterranean world, including Mycenaean Greece and Levantine city-states, is known primarily through archaeology and later tradition. Specific year-by-year precision is not achievable for most regions.

graph_edges.note: Ramesses II's military campaigns in the Levant led to the confrontation with the Hittites at the Battle of Kadesh (c. 1275 BCE).
